summary: An 1700-1721 conflict in which Charles XII's Sweden fought a coalition led by Peter the Great's Russia, joined by Denmark and Poland-Saxony under Augustus the Strong. Charles won an early surprise victory in a blizzard at the Battle of Narva, but the war's turning point came at the 1709 Battle of Poltava, where Peter the Great decisively defeated the Swedish army; Charles then fled to Ottoman territory, provoking the Pruth River Campaign, before returning years later to die at the Siege of Fredriksten. The war also saw Russia's first major naval victory at the Battle of Gangut and the brutal Russian occupation of Finland known as the Great Wrath. It ended with the 1721 Treaty of Nystad, which transferred Ingria and Karelia to Russia and marked Russia's ascendancy over Sweden as the dominant power in the Baltic.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: In giveaways this is identified as the 1700-1721 war in which Charles XII's Sweden was defeated by a coalition led by Peter the Great's Russia, joined by Denmark and Poland-Saxony. It is also accepted as the Second Northern War, and clues consistently frame it as the conflict that ended Sweden's status as the dominant Baltic power and inaugurated Russia's.

clues.clue: 1700-1721 war fought by Charles XII's Sweden against a coalition led by Peter the Great's Russia (joined by Denmark and Poland-Saxony under Augustus the Strong)
examples: name this war in which Peter the Great led a coalition against Charles XII's Sweden.

clues.clue: Ended with the 1721 Treaty of Nystad; marked Russia's ascendancy over Sweden as the dominant Baltic power and the start of Sweden's "Age of Liberty"
examples: name this war ended by the Treaty of Nystad in 1721, in which Charles XII led a Swedish army against Russia.
which ended with Peter the Great's Russia replacing Sweden as the dominant Baltic power.

works.name: Causes, Early Campaigns & Augustus the Strong's Ouster
works.description: While negotiating the anti-Swedish alliance, Peter the Great and Augustus the Strong reportedly competed to see who could bend the most silverware with their bare hands. Early in the war, Sweden's elite Drabant Corps used a smoke screen while crossing the Duna River to strike Augustus's Saxon forces, and a plot by ambassadors Erik Sparre and Karl Gyllenborg drew Britain into the conflict. After the Battle of Fraustadt, the 1706 Treaty of Altranstadt forced Augustus the Strong to renounce the Polish throne (briefly replaced by Stanislaw Leszczynski) and extradite Johann Reinhold Patkul, who was charged with treason for defecting and executed by being broken on the wheel. Sweden's defeat also cost it the Baltic provinces of Ingria and Livonia and the Sound Dues exemption it had won in the earlier Torstenson War.

clues.clue: The Battle of Fraustadt led to the Treaty of Altranstadt (1706), which forced Augustus the Strong to renounce the Polish throne and extradite Johann Reinhold Patkul
examples: After this war's Battle of Fraustadt, the Treaty of Altranstadt was signed to force Augustus the Strong to abdicate his throne.
During this war, the treaty of Altranstadt forced Augustus the Strong to renounce the Polish throne.

clues.clue: Johann Reinhold Patkul was charged with treason for defecting during the war and executed by being broken on the wheel
examples: After being charged with treason for defecting during this war, Johann Patkul was crushed on a breaking wheel.

clues.clue: Sweden's defeat cost it its Sound Dues exemption (won in the earlier Torstenson War) and the provinces of Ingria and Livonia
examples: The losing side in this conflict lost its exemption from the Sound Dues and the provinces of Ingria and Livonia.
Sweden obtained an exemption from the Sound Dues after victory in the Torstenson War, which was lost after its defeat in this conflict.

works.name: Battle of Narva
works.description: Early in the war, Charles XII's Swedish army won a surprise victory in a blizzard at Narva, relieving a Russian siege of the city. It was Charles's first major triumph of the war, fought while Peter the Great's army was still poorly trained.

clues.clue: Charles XII's Swedish forces won a surprise victory during a blizzard here, relieving a Russian siege of the city
examples: A king in this war won a battle during a blizzard at Narva, but fled to the Ottoman court following the Battle of Poltava.
the Swedes under Charles won a victory during a blizzard at this battle, which was fought to relieve a siege of the namesake city.

works.name: Battle of Poltava
works.description: The war's decisive turning point, this 1709 battle saw Peter the Great's Russian army crush Charles XII's Swedish forces, ending Sweden's dominance in the Baltic. Ahead of the battle, Russian forces intercepted and defeated a supply column led by General Adam Lewenhaupt at the Battle of Lesnaya, depriving Charles's main army of reinforcements. During the battle itself, Cossack hetman Ivan Mazepa defected to the Swedish side after learning Peter intended to replace him with Alexander Menshikov, and the Georgian prince Alexander of Imereti was captured; Russia's first field marshal, Boris Sheremetev, defeated Swedish Marshal Carl Gustav Rehnskiold alongside Menshikov. The battle was later the subject of a painting by Alexander von Kotzebue depicting Swedish soldiers retreating across a snow-covered river.

clues.clue: This 1709 battle was the war's decisive turning point, in which Peter the Great's army crushed Charles XII's forces
examples: This war's turning point came when Charles XII's forces were decisively defeated by Peter the Great at the Battle of Poltava.
what war's decisive Battle of Poltava marked the ascendancy of Russia over Sweden in the Baltic region?

clues.clue: Cossack hetman Ivan Mazepa defected to the Swedish side after learning Peter the Great intended to replace him with Alexander Menshikov, then fled with the defeated Swedes to the Ottoman Empire, where he died
examples: A Cossack defection did not prevent one ruler from losing this conflict's Battle of Poltava.
Ivan Mazepa died after joining the forces of this war's losing side and fleeing with them to the Ottoman Empire.

clues.clue: Russian forces defeated a supply column led by General Adam Lewenhaupt at the Battle of Lesnaya shortly beforehand, depriving Charles XII's main army of reinforcements

examples: Boris Sheremetev, Russia's first field marshal, defeated Marshal Rehnskiold at this battle alongside Prince Menshikov.

works.name: Charles XII's Ottoman Exile & the Pruth River Campaign
works.description: After Poltava, Charles XII fled to the Ottoman-controlled town of Bender in present-day Moldova, where his entourage's lavish spending led the Ottomans to nickname him "Iron-Head Charles." In 1713, an Ottoman operation there briefly captured Charles in an incident known as the "kalabalik." When the Ottomans refused to hand him over, Peter the Great launched the Pruth River Campaign, allied with Prince Dmitrie Cantemir; Peter's forces were surrounded by Grand Vizier Baltadji at the Battle of Stanilesti but received surprisingly lenient peace terms.

clues.clue: Charles XII was besieged/holed up at the Ottoman town of Bender in present-day Moldova, where his lavish spending led the Ottomans to nickname him "Iron-Head Charles"
examples: Charles XII was holed up at this town in present-day Moldova, where his entourage ran up such heavy expenses that the Ottomans began to call him "Iron-Head Charles."

clues.clue: An Ottoman operation at Bender, known as the "kalabalik," briefly captured/imprisoned Charles XII
examples: In the Moldavian town of Bender, an Ottoman operation during this war ended with the capture of Charles XII.
One ruler in this war was briefly taken prisoner after an event known as the "kalabalik."

clues.clue: When the Ottomans refused to surrender Charles XII, Peter the Great launched the Pruth River Campaign allied with Prince Dmitrie Cantemir, whose forces were surrounded by Grand Vizier Baltadji at the Battle of Stanilesti but received surprisingly lenient peace terms
examples: Peter the Great launched the Pruth River Campaign after the Ottomans refused to give up Charles XII, who had fled after losing the Battle of Poltava to Peter during this war.

works.name: Battle of Gangut
works.description: This 1714 engagement near the Hanko Peninsula was Russia's first major naval victory, won by Admiral Fyodor Apraksin. The Swedish flagship Elefanten was captured and Admiral Ehrenskiold taken prisoner after refusing to surrender; ever since, the Russian navy has always kept a ship named Gangut in commemoration.

clues.clue: This 1714 naval victory near the Hanko Peninsula, led by Admiral Fyodor Apraksin, was one side's first major naval victory of the war
examples: Russian success at Poltava paved the way for this 1714 battle during the Great Northern War, a Russian naval victory for Admiral Fyodor Apraksin near the Hanko Peninsula.
The victorious side of this war won its first naval victory at the Battle of Gangut before concluding it with the Treaty of Nystad.

clues.clue: The Swedish flagship Elefanten was captured and Admiral Ehrenskiold taken prisoner after refusing to surrender
examples: The largest ship of one side in this conflict was positioned to block the approaching forces who eventually captured the Elefanten, taking the admiral as a prisoner of war from his own ship.
One side in this battle had Admiral Ehrenskiold taken as a prisoner of war aboard the Elefanten after his refusal to surrender.

clues.clue: Ever since the battle, the Russian navy has always kept a ship named Gangut in commemoration
examples: To commemorate the victory during this war, the navy of the winning side of that battle always has a vessel named Gangut.
The Russian navy has always included a ship named Gangut ever since their naval victory at Gangut in this war.

works.name: Battle of Gadebusch & the Carolean Death March
works.description: Using "Ga-Pa" tactics, in which musketeers marched to within fifty paces of the enemy, let the rear ranks fire, then charged rapidly to twenty meters for a volley followed closely by bayonet warfare, Swedish forces under Magnus Stenbock defeated Jacob von Flemming's cavalry at the Battle of Gadebusch. That same Swedish army was later decimated when forced to retreat through Norway's Tydal mountains in a grueling action remembered as the Carolean Death March.

clues.clue: Swedish musketeers used "Ga-Pa" tactics (marching to fifty paces, letting rear ranks fire, then charging to twenty meters for a volley and bayonet charge) to defeat Jacob von Flemming's cavalry at this battle
examples: One side in this conflict used a strategy by which its musketeers were trained to close to fifty paces, allow the rear ranks to fire, then charge rapidly to 20 meters for a musket volley followed closely by bayonet warfare.
That strategy, known as "Ga-Pa Tactics," was used to defeat Jacob von Flemming's cavalry during this conflict at the Battle of Gadebusch.

clues.clue: The victorious Swedish army was later decimated when forced to retreat through the Tydal mountains in an action called the Carolean Death March
examples: that army in this conflict was later decimated when it was forced to retreat through the Tydal mountains in an action called the Carolean Death March.

works.name: The Great Wrath
works.description: During Russia's occupation of Finland late in the war, hundreds of civilians were massacred in an incident known as "Murder Friday," part of the broader devastation of Finnish provinces remembered as the Great Wrath. Refugees displaced by the war also spread a plague outbreak that coincided with the most extreme of the era's "Great Frosts."

clues.clue: Hundreds of civilians were massacred in "Murder Friday," part of the devastation of a Finnish province during the Russian occupation known as the Great Wrath
examples: Hundreds of civilians were massacred during this conflict's Murder Friday, part of the devastation of one province in the Great Wrath.

clues.clue: Refugees displaced by the war spread a plague outbreak that coincided with the most extreme of the "Great Frosts"
examples: Refugees displaced by this conflict spread an outbreak of plague that coincided with the most extreme of the Great Frosts.

works.name: End of the War: Death of Charles XII & Treaty of Nystad
works.description: Years after fleeing to Ottoman Moldavia, Charles XII returned to Sweden's remaining war effort and was shot in the head and killed during the Siege of Fredriksten in Norway; the site where he fell was marked by a cross of two strips of peat, an incident described in the opening paragraph of a cultural history by Orlando Figes. The war formally ended with the 1721 Treaty of Nystad, under which Ingria and Karelia were transferred to Russia while Sweden was returned most of Finland.

clues.clue: Charles XII was shot in the head and killed during the Siege of Fredriksten years after fleeing to Ottoman Moldavia
examples: Years after the leader of one side during this war escaped to Turkish Moldavia, he was shot in the head and killed during the Siege of Fredriksten.

clues.clue: An incident in which a man marked the site of Charles XII's death by arranging two strips of peat into a cross opens a cultural history by Orlando Figes
examples: An incident in this war in which a man marked a site by arranging two strips of peat into a cross is described in the first paragraph of a cultural history by Orlando Figes.

clues.clue: The 1721 Treaty of Nystad ended the war, transferring Ingria and Karelia to Russia while returning most of Finland to Sweden
examples: Ingria and Karelia were transferred to the winning side after this war, while the losing side was returned most of Finland.
name this war ended by the Treaty of Nystad in 1721

comprehensive_summary: The Great Northern War (1700-1721) pitted Charles XII's Sweden against a coalition led by Peter the Great's Russia and joined by Denmark and Poland-Saxony under Augustus the Strong. While negotiating their alliance, Peter and Augustus reportedly competed at bending silverware with their bare hands; early campaigns saw Sweden's Drabant Corps cross the Duna River under a smoke screen, and Charles XII won a stunning blizzard victory relieving the siege of Narva. Sweden's fortunes turned after the Battle of Fraustadt and the resulting Treaty of Altranstadt (1706), which forced Augustus to renounce the Polish throne and extradite Johann Reinhold Patkul, later executed by being broken on the wheel; Sweden's ultimate defeat cost it the Sound Dues exemption and the provinces of Ingria and Livonia. The war's decisive turning point came at the 1709 Battle of Poltava: after Russian forces intercepted a relief column under Adam Lewenhaupt at Lesnaya, Peter the Great crushed Charles XII's army, during which Cossack hetman Ivan Mazepa's defection failed to save the Swedes and Russia's Boris Sheremetev and Alexander Menshikov defeated Marshal Rehnskiold. Charles XII fled to the Ottoman town of Bender in Moldova, where lavish spending earned him the nickname "Iron-Head Charles" and where he was briefly seized in the 1713 "kalabalik"; when the Ottomans refused to surrender him, Peter's Pruth River Campaign nearly ended in disaster before securing lenient peace terms. Russia also won its first major naval victory at the 1714 Battle of Gangut, capturing the Swedish flagship Elefanten, while Swedish forces under "Ga-Pa" tactics triumphed at Gadebusch only to be devastated afterward on the Carolean Death March through the Tydal mountains. Russia's brutal occupation of Finland, the Great Wrath, included the Murder Friday massacre and a refugee-driven plague outbreak. Charles XII was eventually shot dead at the Siege of Fredriksten in Norway, and the war concluded with the 1721 Treaty of Nystad, which transferred Ingria and Karelia to Russia, returned most of Finland to Sweden, and marked Russia's rise as the Baltic's dominant power as Sweden entered its "Age of Liberty."
recursive_suggestions: Battle of Gangut -- recurs as an independent bonus-part answerline (2x: IQBT 2024, NASAT 2016) with a dense cluster (Elefanten, Admiral Ehrenskiold, Apraksin); candidate for a standalone topic.

Battle of Poltava -- also recurs as its own bonus-part answerline; an extremely famous, dense battle that could support a standalone page, though its core facts were kept here given its centrality to the war's narrative.
Ivan Mazepa -- recurring bonus-part answerline (Cossack hetman, Ukrainian national hero) with a dense independent biographical cluster; candidate for a standalone History/Social Science figure page.

Augustus the Strong (Augustus II) -- recurs as his own bonus-part answerline with rich non-war biographical detail (Grunes Gewolbe, Zwinger Palace, King of Poland/Elector of Saxony); candidate for a standalone figure page.
Pruth River Campaign -- recurs as its own bonus-part answerline; a distinct Russo-Ottoman conflict that could support its own topic.
Torstenson War -- mentioned only as background to the Sound Dues bonus; a distinct earlier 17th-century Baltic war, excluded here.

Taganrog / John Paul Jones / Hubertusberg -- unrelated bonus parts (Peter the Great's later Black Sea naval base, an 18th-century American naval officer, and a Seven Years' War treaty site) swept into the same bonus sets by loose theme; excluded as off-topic.
